item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Last thing, how are we to know that his extreme longevity doesn't come from his anomalous qualities somehow converting the unrealized life potential of the people that "died" into additional lifespan?

This is basically what my intent was, although replacing "unrealized life potential" with "youth". It was absorbing victim's youth, since that's a major part of your identity. I imagine it was able to stay alive in Foundation containment by leveraging the fact that its power works on fictional characters, which it would create.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The more I read this article the more I actually like it. Even though the "good" Dr Clouzot seems very one dimensional there are a lot of people in this world that are exactly like he is, they only think in terms of how they can turn a current situation to their advantage. His character was true to point the entire time.

Another interesting thing is their "immortality", more like extreme longevity than traditional immortality. The extent of his powers are not understood, and probably never will be, but it really seems like this could have been him dying because he made a conscious code specifically NOT to learn about Dr Clouzot this making the exchange unequal and the anomaly taking the bring of the exchange.

Last thing, how are we to know that his extreme longevity doesn't come from his anomalous qualities somehow converting the unrealized life potential of the people that "died" into additional lifespan? We delivered don't know enough about how the people died from his anomalous abilities, they could just faded away.

Just my thoughts, and an upvote. 😀

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ending was suprisingly posiive, I expected much worse. I expected the condition to pass to the doctor investigating or something of shorts, along with the imortality.

For sticking his nose were he shouldn't, the doctor becomes the SCP and has no hopes of alerting anyone without dying, his very existence forgotten.All he can do is forever bare the burden he unkowingly assumed and hope no one else does what he did

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To address two points of this:

  • I have a complicated answer of how 5041 is able to leverage its power to resist aging. It involves some properties of 5041 that are only implied in this, and not fully elucidated.
  • A lot of people have had an issue with the village line, so I have removed that and substituted the more generic "large number of victims".
    • The answer to the illness question is a fault of modern medicine. The reason 5041 dies to Clouzot's diabetes is because Clouzot is being treated for it. Before the development of modern medicine, the people with diseases that'd prove fatal in a short period of time, well, died. They would either die of those disease before 5041 could die of them or die to 5041.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’ve got to neutral/novote this one.

I was ABSOLUTELY into the mystery the whole time. Your wind up was excellent. I compared the read to 5031 (which I adore) in my head. But two things took me out of this:

One: Clouzot's character was thin. The type of person who types “I’m going to get PROMOTED for this.” is kind of one-dimensional.

Two: Revealing that 5041’s power so to speak is “I get parts of the people who know me and they get parts of me” is neat and justifies the conprocs, but immortality on top of it annoys me (Did he steal the immortality from someone using his power?) as well as killing a whole village but being taken out by diabetes from one researcher (didn’t anyone in that village have an illness that would have taken him out?)

I enjoyed the mystery portion so much, but Clouzot and the ending didn’t work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough, that makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 While I enjoyed the story, the village definitely stands out. It's mentioned as disappeared, as though it vanished into thin air, not that all the residents died, so this is maybe lacking some clarification or detail. Where the bodies found? Did the corpses pass on the anomaly? How could such an anomaly even be contained?

To some degree, I'm keeping the details of what happened to the village vague. It's not a particularly important matter, but I can't go into full detail about it in the SCP, and it's better to make it clear that there are still some things that Clouzot's theory (which is what you're reading in the second offset) didn't account for.

Also, there appears to be no ill affect from the Dr. Cocteau interview, other than the vague hint that 5041 is in some pain, was he also fired for interfering with a stable anomaly?

Lebeau's Interview intentionally only uses first person plural pronouns and doesn't reveal any personal information about Lebeau in it, so he was safe from the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, see, the thing is that researching the anomaly was not actually his job. Clouzot's job as the HMCL is to supervise containment and make sure that is carried out well. Research is only necessary as a means to refine the Containment Procedures, which he notes have been in place for a while and are unlikely to be amended. Doctor Lebeau was on the job for decades and never felt it necessary to do all this research and form a theory.

Further, his actions are in violation of the spirit, if not the exact letter of the Containment Procedures. While there's nothing that prevented him, he should have picked up on this sooner. Further, a review of his logs — which would be conducted just to see if there was enough for an indictment — would indicate he wasn't doing this for the benefit of containment, but because he was angling for a promotion, going so far as to ignore the anomaly when it begged him to stop. That's the malicious intent right there, a reckless disregard for the person's concerns. He brushes them away immediately without really considering why he was asked to stop.

Considering the nature of the Foundation's work, an anomaly dying because of complete lack of knowledge, and no malicious intent or mala praxis, is one of those things that would happen eventually.

I mean yeah, the charge against him at the end is manslaughter, not murder. There's a legal distinction between the two and the charge was chosen intentionally. Manslaughter doesn't require malicious intent. They're bringing him up on charges of negligent homicide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did like the anomaly itself, but the way the discovery is presented didn't do it for me. I can't pin-point the specifics as to why, but as mentioned above, Clouzot's character might have something to do with it. Felt less like I was trying to learn about the ability alongside the character, and more like I was just waiting and soaking in the info, if that makes sense.

I also don't understand why he got punished by the Ethics Committee. He was given the assignment and didn't break any rules, just used archived information to get a better idea on the anomaly, which is standard Foundation MO. Why would you assign someone to an SCP and then tell them off for doing their job? The death of the SCP was also not his fault (practically speaking) since there was almost no information about its abilities and no way to know this would happen. Considering the nature of the Foundation's work, an anomaly dying because of complete lack of knowledge, and no malicious intent or mala praxis, is one of those things that would happen eventually.

It's gonna be a -1 from me.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
xX_RavenClaw_Xx, this is a violation of Rule Zero: Don't be a dick.

There are ways of expressing your dislike for an article without having to resort to such rude behavior.

Do not do this again.

item.title: cliche
item.content:encoded: wow a scp that no one knows why its contained and the page is more like a tale than an actual scp because edgy = cool am i right guys

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 While I enjoyed the story, the village definitely stands out. It's mentioned as disappeared, as though it vanished into thin air, not that all the residents died, so this is maybe lacking some clarification or detail. Where the bodies found? Did the corpses pass on the anomaly? How could such an anomaly even be contained?

Also, there appears to be no ill affect from the Dr. Cocteau interview, other than the vague hint that 5041 is in some pain, was he also fired for interfering with a stable anomaly?

Thanks for a good read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Clouzot learned a valuable lesson - you can get what you want and still not be happy.

I wonder if death was a welcome relief for the prisoner. DKA is an uncomfortable condition, but probably no worse than being locked up for three hundred years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always think the Renaissance is much later than it actually is. I've changed that to Belle Époque France to imply the furniture/room was updated around when the Foundation was formed and have an accurate historical era mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the ideas here. Secret prisoners that were detained without their crimes or identities ever recorded are a fascinating piece of history, and this does a wonderful job of embracing that intrigue. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 18th century isn't the Renaissance. Is this intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a brilliant piece. The mystery is thoroughly intriguing and hangs in the space the entire way, making the ending very satisfying. It comes in just a bit suddenly, but the logic makes enough sense: if nothing can be known by either party, nothing can happen. Easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You appear to have missed about 90% of the article. There's a large, bolded collapsible at the bottom you have not read.

Every question you have is answered by the actual article. That being said…

Exactly what are those provisions? What are the procedures if said person is allowed access?

These questions are answered in the second paragraph of the containment procedures.

The description is also lacking. How did it come contained for 300 years? What kind of humanoid is it?

Putting aside the fact that these questions are answered, the fact that it's lacking is *the entire point* of that description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Response
– hide block

Thank you for reading this, I'm glad you enjoyed the story. I've made a few revisions to this typoes you pointed out.

Is the "SCC" abbreviation used anywhere else? I've never seen it before and it kinda stands out as unusual.

Nope, made it up here. It's just an acronym, so I don't get what you mean when you say it feels unusual.

The tone of this seems a bit iffy. What are the procedures if someone does do this? Even if its reason isn't known to staff, I doubt they would let it remain so imprecise.

Added a line about amnestic treatment if that's violated.

What sort of entertainment media doesn't have any characters? It seems fairly restrictive; even music without lyrics can "refer to" an individual by its history. Perhaps stating here what entertainment media is allowed would be more appropriate. The Foundation errs on the side of caution with these things.

The refer to is a bit restrictive, I've changed that to depictions only. Examples of appropriate media would be poetry that doesn't have a speaker/subject, simple video games like Pong and Tetris, nature documentaries with the narration removed, instrumental music, or artistic materials. This is a very broad category, and it's really up to the containment staff to decide what's appropriate. Putting firm guidelines down is too restrictive.

The prisoner is to be allowed the materials with which to write and paint.

I'd consider these to be entertainment materials.

This needs elaboration or redaction, especially the bit about "otherwise cease to exist".

Looks like a quote got skipped, but there is a psuedo-redaction here, in that the Foundation doesn't have a full understanding of how that process occurs. They know a village is wiped out from existence, but that happened two hundred years before they even existed. The Foundation can't elaborate any further.

There's no link back to the original page once you're on the updated one.

Why does the main link take me to an out-of-date entry?

These are mostly conceits of listpages as a narrative device. For what it's worth, the first isn't important (and your back button exists). For the second, the intended logic is that you are reading this document as it updates. That's not particularly evident, but that isn't a unique problem to this article, and dozens of listpage articles have the same problem. It's not something I'm going to fix.

How did he kill a whole town with these powers, yet a single guy having diabetes killed him?

Most people in 1713 weren't walking around with illnesses that could result in them dying in a few hours/days without treatment. That's something modern medicine allows for that wouldn't have happened in the past. The people in the village were healthy enough to live for another week or so — but 5041 kills them first, and any ailments they might have had fade away. There's also the fact that it could have gotten medical treatment in the past, which isn't the case in total isolation in a cell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did you read the rest of the article? Everything you said is explained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do enjoy the effort you've put into the containment procedure, but some doesn't make it.

SCC-01 should not be accessed by any individual outside the specific provisions allowed by the containment procedures

Exactly what are those provisions? What are the procedures if said person is allowed access?

The description is also lacking. How did it come contained for 300 years? What kind of humanoid is it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the "SCC" abbreviation used anywhere else? I've never seen it before and it kinda stands out as unusual.

The only room into which containment staff are allowed to enter is the antechamber, and only to deliver food or other provisions to SCP-5041

The second half of this isn't a complete sentence, so the comma before "and" isn't correct.

Under no circumstances should the interior of SCC-01 be looked into.

The tone of this seems a bit iffy. What are the procedures if someone does do this? Even if its reason isn't known to staff, I doubt they would let it remain so imprecise.

+ show block
– hide block

Entertainment media given to SCP-5041 should not contain depictions of or references to any individuals or characters, real or fictional.

What sort of entertainment media doesn't have any characters? It seems fairly restrictive; even music without lyrics can "refer to" an individual by its history. Perhaps stating here what entertainment media is allowed would be more appropriate. The Foundation errs on the side of caution with these things.

The prisoner is to be allowed the materials with which to write and paint.

Why didn't this make it to containment procedures?

There is no way to touch me that does cut you to shreds.

Should that be "doesn't"?

This needs elaboration or redaction, especially the bit about "otherwise cease to exist".

There's no link back to the original page once you're on the updated one.

Why does the main link take me to an out-of-date entry?

How did he kill a whole town with these powers, yet a single guy having diabetes killed him?

I really like the whole concept. There are a lot of articles where Foundation personnel are rewarded for jacking with stuff and encouraged to do research on subjects, but that's not what they're about. This is: using a containment procedure that makes no sense, without knowing why, because it works. It's a great story and I can tell there was a ton of work put into it. It just suffers from some tone issues; I tried pointing out where, but I'm having a hard time putting my finger on it exactly.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unfortunate that the characterization doesn't work for you, I'll see if there's anything I can do to shore that up.

I also find it hard to believe the effects of the anomaly could somehow kill a village. That needs a better explanation.

It's pretty simple. Every person in the village was affected by 5041 and died, because late-stage exposure to it is fatal. Affected individuals can spread the effect — you can learn about 5041 by seeing traits that appear in affected individuals, which will infect you yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't really do anything for me. Despite the themes of mystery inherent to the narrative, I never found myself searching for character motives or questioning Clouzot's actions. Everything he does lacks subtlety.

So much for a dead-end career — I'll be able to leverage this hard. Promotions, advancement, it's all opening up now for me.

The character depth here is unsatisfyingly shallow. The mystery of 5041 itself isn't much better in places; it's missing that element of "being personal", of having some real hook beyond the initial oddity of an object collected from a predecessor organization.

I also find it hard to believe the effects of the anomaly could somehow kill a village. That needs a better explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The earliest version of this article looked radically, radically different. It was initially inspired by the case of "Herman Emmanuel Fankem", a Canadian prisoner who is not actually named that. His real name and identity is unknown. The idea of a prisoner without an identity or reason to be in prison fascinated me, and I started a draft along those lines.

Some time later, I learned of the Man in the the Iron Mask, a French prisoner from the 1600s with both unknown identity and crime. This fascinated me, and I started working from there again, scrapping everything I had before.

I've written a few mystery SCPs in the past without a clear explanation or authorial answer, and I wanted to avoid that this time. I thought of an anomaly with a good reason for the Foundation to be unaware, and went from there, as well as putting a few bureaucratic reasons in (I've been a fan of the Foundation inheriting anomalies from precursors and not fully understanding them for a while).

The second draft of this looked rather different. It had the same ConProcs and Description, and two addenda — the Estate noir letter and the Interview. But it didn't have any mention of Dr. Cocteau, or his story.

MaliceAforethought looked at it, and told me that while the story was put together in a new way, there wasn't really anything there he hadn't seen before. It was a collection of familiar tropes with a new coat of paint. I was already feeling that the draft was missing something, and wasn't fond of it. Back to the drawing board.

Enter Dr. Clouzot. As I was thinking, I came up with the idea for a Foundation researcher doing the investigation into SCP-5041 and in the process killing it. The cause of death went through a few variations, but settled on the diabetes: firmly Clouzot's fault, truly accidental and oh so mundane.
